cards.definition: a large section of the Earth's crust and upper mantle that moves slowly on the mantle
a zone where two tectonic plates meet — constructive, destructive, or conservative
where two plates move apart — magma rises to fill the gap, creating new crust and volcanoes
where two plates collide — oceanic crust is subducted under continental crust, causing volcanoes and earthquakes
where two plates slide past each other — no crust created or destroyed, but major earthquakes occur
a sudden violent shaking of the ground caused by the movement of tectonic plates
the point underground where an earthquake begins
the point on the Earth's surface directly above the focus of an earthquake
a logarithmic scale measuring the magnitude of an earthquake — each point = 10× more powerful
a series of large ocean waves caused by undersea earthquakes or volcanic eruptions

questions.question: What is a tectonic plate?
options: A) A layer of rock in the ocean
B) A large section of Earth's crust that moves on the mantle
C) A type of earthquake fault
D) The surface of the Earth's core
questions.explanation: Tectonic plates are huge slabs of rock that make up the Earth's crust and upper mantle. They move very slowly — a few centimetres per year.

questions.question: What happens at a constructive plate boundary?
options: A) Plates collide and one is subducted
B) Plates slide past each other
C) Plates move apart — magma rises to form new crust and volcanoes
D) Plates merge into one
questions.explanation: At constructive (divergent) boundaries, plates pull apart — magma rises to fill the gap, forming new oceanic crust, mid-ocean ridges, and volcanoes.

questions.question: What happens at a destructive plate boundary?
options: A) Two plates slide past each other
B) Oceanic crust is subducted under continental crust — causing volcanoes and earthquakes
C) Plates move apart — new crust forms
D) No earthquakes occur
questions.explanation: At destructive (convergent) boundaries, denser oceanic crust is forced under lighter continental crust (subduction) — causing deep-focus earthquakes and explosive volcanoes.

questions.question: What is the epicentre of an earthquake?
options: A) The point underground where the earthquake begins
B) The point on the surface directly above the focus
C) The most damaged area
D) The edge of a tectonic plate
questions.explanation: The epicentre is the point on the Earth's surface directly above the focus. Damage is usually greatest here.

questions.question: What causes a tsunami?
options: A) A hurricane moving over the sea
B) Undersea earthquakes or volcanic eruptions displacing large volumes of water
C) Very strong coastal winds
D) Rapid sea level rise
questions.explanation: Tsunamis are caused by sudden displacement of the ocean floor — usually by undersea earthquakes. The displaced water travels as waves across the ocean.

questions.question: What does the Richter scale measure?
options: A) The depth of an earthquake focus
B) The damage caused by an earthquake
C) The magnitude (energy released) of an earthquake
D) The duration of shaking
questions.explanation: The Richter scale measures seismic wave amplitude. It is logarithmic — a magnitude 7 earthquake is 10 times more powerful than a magnitude 6.

questions.question: What type of plate boundary causes earthquakes but NOT volcanoes?
options: A) Constructive
B) Destructive
C) Conservative
D) Subduction
questions.explanation: At conservative (transform) boundaries, plates slide past each other. No crust is created or destroyed — so no volcanoes, but extreme earthquakes occur (e.g. San Andreas Fault).

questions.question: What is the focus of an earthquake?
options: A) The surface point where damage is greatest
B) The underground point where the earthquake originates
C) The scale used to measure earthquakes
D) The type of rock at a plate boundary
questions.explanation: The focus is the point underground where rocks fracture and release energy. Shallow-focus earthquakes cause more surface damage than deep ones.

questions.question: What forms at constructive plate boundaries?
options: A) Deep ocean trenches
B) Mountain ranges
C) Mid-ocean ridges and new oceanic crust
D) Fold mountains
questions.explanation: At constructive boundaries, magma rises and solidifies to form new oceanic crust — creating mid-ocean ridges like the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.

questions.question: What is subduction?
options: A) Plates moving apart
B) Plates sliding past each other
C) Denser oceanic crust being forced under lighter continental crust
D) Magma rising to form new crust
questions.explanation: Subduction occurs at destructive boundaries — denser oceanic plate is forced beneath the less dense continental plate, melting in the mantle and causing volcanic eruptions.

questions.question: Tectonic plates move several metres per year.
questions.explanation: Tectonic plates move only a few centimetres per year — about the same rate as fingernails grow.

questions.question: Constructive plate boundaries produce new oceanic crust.
questions.explanation: At constructive boundaries, plates move apart and magma rises to fill the gap — forming new crust.

questions.question: Conservative plate boundaries are the most likely to produce volcanoes.
questions.explanation: Conservative boundaries produce earthquakes but NOT volcanoes — no crust is created or destroyed.

questions.question: The epicentre is the underground point where an earthquake starts.
questions.explanation: The focus is underground. The epicentre is the point on the SURFACE directly above the focus.

questions.question: Tsunamis are caused by undersea earthquakes or eruptions.
questions.explanation: Any event that suddenly displaces a large volume of water can cause a tsunami — most commonly undersea earthquakes.

questions.question: The Richter scale is a linear scale.
questions.explanation: The Richter scale is logarithmic — each step represents a 10-fold increase in earthquake magnitude.

questions.question: At destructive boundaries, oceanic crust is subducted under continental crust.
questions.explanation: Oceanic crust is denser — at destructive boundaries it is forced under lighter continental crust.

questions.question: Volcanoes at destructive boundaries tend to be more explosive than those at constructive boundaries.
questions.explanation: Subducted oceanic crust melts and produces silica-rich magma which is viscous and explosive. Constructive boundary volcanoes have runnier lava and are less explosive.

questions.question: Deep-focus earthquakes cause more surface damage than shallow-focus ones.
questions.explanation: Shallow-focus earthquakes cause MORE surface damage — the energy has less rock to travel through before reaching the surface.

questions.question: Mid-ocean ridges form at constructive plate boundaries.
questions.explanation: Mid-ocean ridges like the Mid-Atlantic Ridge form where plates pull apart and new oceanic crust is created.

questions.question: Tectonic ________ are large sections of Earth's crust that move slowly on the mantle.
questions.explanation: There are about 12 major tectonic plates and several smaller ones.

questions.question: At a ________ plate boundary, plates move apart — magma rises and new crust forms.
questions.explanation: Constructive boundaries produce mid-ocean ridges, gently sloping volcanoes, and shallow earthquakes.

questions.question: At a ________ plate boundary, oceanic crust is subducted under continental crust.
questions.explanation: Destructive boundaries produce explosive volcanoes, deep-focus earthquakes, and ocean trenches.

questions.question: At a ________ plate boundary, plates slide past each other — earthquakes but no volcanoes.
questions.explanation: The San Andreas Fault in California is a famous conservative plate boundary.

questions.question: The ________ is the underground point where an earthquake begins.
questions.explanation: The deeper the focus, the less surface damage is caused.

questions.question: The ________ is the point on the surface directly above the earthquake focus.
questions.explanation: Damage is usually greatest at the epicentre.

questions.question: The ________ scale measures earthquake magnitude — a logarithmic scale.
questions.explanation: Each step on the Richter scale represents a 10-fold increase in energy.

questions.question: A ________ is a series of large waves caused by undersea earthquakes or volcanic eruptions.
questions.explanation: Tsunamis travel at up to 800 km/h across the ocean.

questions.question: ________ occurs when denser oceanic crust is forced under lighter continental crust.
questions.explanation: Subducted crust melts in the mantle — producing explosive volcanic eruptions.

questions.question: Mid-ocean ________ form at constructive boundaries where plates move apart.
questions.explanation: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge runs the length of the Atlantic Ocean.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: plates move apart — new crust forms, gentle volcanoes
oceanic crust subducted — explosive volcanoes, deep earthquakes
plates slide past — earthquakes but no volcanoes
underground point where earthquake begins
large waves caused by undersea earthquake or eruption

cards.definition: a dense forest found near the equator — hot and wet all year, high biodiversity, multiple canopy layers
the variety of plant and animal species in an area — tropical rainforests have the highest biodiversity on Earth
the process by which nutrients are recycled through the ecosystem — in rainforests, nutrients are in the biomass not the soil
the top layer of the rainforest — very tall trees (50m+) above the main canopy, exposed to full sunlight
the main layer of the rainforest (30–40m) — dense leaves absorbing most sunlight, home to most wildlife
the layer below the canopy — adapted to low light; plants have large, dark leaves
the clearing of forest — for agriculture, mining, logging, and settlement — causing habitat loss and climate change
tropical rainforests absorb CO₂ and store carbon in their biomass — deforestation releases this as greenhouse gas
meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs — sustainable forestry, ecotourism
all parts of an ecosystem are linked — changing one component affects others

questions.question: Where are tropical rainforests found?
options: A) Near the poles
B) Near the equator — between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn
C) In temperate regions
D) Only in South America
questions.explanation: Tropical rainforests are found near the equator — where temperatures are consistently high (26–28°C) and rainfall is heavy (2000mm+/year).

questions.question: Why do tropical rainforests have poor soil?
options: A) Too much rain washing nutrients down
B) Nutrients are in the biomass, not the soil — rapid decay and uptake
C) Volcanic ash makes soil infertile
D) High temperatures destroy soil nutrients
questions.explanation: In rainforests, nutrients are recycled so quickly that the soil itself is nutrient-poor. Most nutrients are stored in the living biomass — when vegetation is removed, fertility collapses.

questions.question: What is biodiversity?
options: A) The number of trees in an area
B) The variety of plant and animal species
C) The number of ecosystem layers
D) The amount of rainfall an ecosystem receives
questions.explanation: Biodiversity refers to the variety of species. Tropical rainforests have the highest biodiversity on Earth — housing over 50% of all species on just 6% of land area.

questions.question: What is the emergent layer?
options: A) The forest floor
B) The middle canopy
C) The very tallest trees above the main canopy
D) The understorey
questions.explanation: Emergent trees grow 50m+ and poke above the main canopy. They are exposed to maximum sunlight and wind.

questions.question: Why is deforestation a problem?
options: A) It increases biodiversity
B) It makes soil more fertile
C) It causes habitat loss, releases carbon, and disrupts the water cycle
D) It reduces flooding
questions.explanation: Deforestation causes: species extinction, carbon release (climate change), soil erosion (nutrients lost), reduced transpiration (disrupting rainfall), and indigenous community displacement.

questions.question: What is interdependence in an ecosystem?
options: A) Species competing for the same food
B) All parts of an ecosystem being linked — change one, it affects others
C) Animals depending on humans for food
D) Plants depending only on sunlight
questions.explanation: Interdependence means the species and physical environment are all connected — remove one element and it can cascade through the whole ecosystem.

questions.question: Why do rainforest soils become infertile quickly after deforestation?
options: A) The trees take nutrients with them
B) The soil was always infertile — only biomass held nutrients
C) Rain immediately erodes the topsoil
D) Sunlight destroys soil bacteria
questions.explanation: Nutrients in rainforests are stored in the biomass — when trees are cleared, nutrient recycling stops. Tropical rain quickly washes away the thin topsoil.

questions.question: What is sustainable management of rainforests?
options: A) Cutting all trees at once to maximise profit
B) Burning forests for agriculture
C) Selective logging, ecotourism, and education to protect forests for the future
D) Converting rainforests to farmland permanently
questions.explanation: Sustainable management includes selective logging (not clear-cutting), ecotourism, conservation agreements, and supporting indigenous communities.

questions.question: How do tropical rainforests affect climate?
options: A) They increase CO₂ by releasing it constantly
B) They store carbon and produce oxygen — helping regulate global climate
C) They have no effect on global climate
D) They increase temperature globally
questions.explanation: Rainforests are carbon sinks — they absorb CO₂ through photosynthesis. They also contribute to global rainfall through transpiration.

questions.question: Which layer of the rainforest receives the least sunlight?
options: A) Emergent layer
B) Canopy
C) Understorey
D) Forest floor
questions.explanation: The forest floor receives less than 2% of sunlight — it is dark and humid. Plants here have adaptations like large leaves to capture the little light that filters through.

questions.question: Tropical rainforests are found near the poles.
questions.explanation: Tropical rainforests are found near the equator — between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn.

questions.question: Rainforest soils are very fertile.
questions.explanation: Rainforest soils are actually nutrient-poor — most nutrients are stored in the living biomass, not the soil.

questions.question: Tropical rainforests have the highest biodiversity on Earth.
questions.explanation: Rainforests cover about 6% of land but house over 50% of all species.

questions.question: The emergent layer is at the bottom of the rainforest.
questions.explanation: The emergent layer is the HIGHEST — the tallest trees that poke above the main canopy.

questions.question: Deforestation releases stored carbon as a greenhouse gas.
questions.explanation: Trees store carbon in their biomass. When burned or decomposed after deforestation, this carbon is released as CO₂.

questions.question: All layers of the rainforest receive equal amounts of sunlight.
questions.explanation: Light decreases significantly with depth — the forest floor receives less than 2% of available sunlight.

questions.question: Interdependence means changing one part of an ecosystem affects others.
questions.explanation: Ecosystems are complex webs — removing one species or changing one physical factor can cascade through the whole system.

questions.question: Sustainable management includes ecotourism and selective logging.
questions.explanation: Sustainable management aims to use resources without permanently destroying them — allowing future use.

questions.question: Nutrient cycling in rainforests stores nutrients in the soil.
questions.explanation: In rainforests, nutrients are rapidly recycled through the biomass — NOT stored in the soil.

questions.question: Deforestation increases biodiversity by opening up the canopy.
questions.explanation: Deforestation destroys habitat — causing species extinction and dramatically reducing biodiversity.

questions.question: Tropical rainforests are found near the ________ — between the two Tropics.
questions.explanation: The equatorial climate provides consistent heat and rainfall year-round.

questions.question: ________ is the variety of plant and animal species — highest in tropical rainforests.
questions.explanation: Tropical rainforests house over 50% of all species on just 6% of land.

questions.question: The ________ layer contains the tallest trees — over 50m — above the main canopy.
questions.explanation: Emergent trees are exposed to maximum sunlight and wind.

questions.question: The ________ is the main layer — dense leaves absorbing most sunlight.
questions.explanation: The canopy is home to most of the rainforest's wildlife.

questions.question: ________ is clearing forest for farming, logging, or development.
questions.explanation: Deforestation causes habitat loss, carbon release, and soil erosion.

questions.question: Rainforests are poor in ________ because nutrients are stored in the biomass, not the soil.
questions.explanation: When trees are removed, the thin, infertile soil is quickly washed away.

questions.question: Rainforests are ________ stores — they absorb CO₂ through photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Deforestation releases stored carbon as CO₂ — contributing to climate change.

questions.question: ________ management includes selective logging and ecotourism.
questions.explanation: Sustainable management aims to meet present needs without destroying resources for the future.

questions.question: ________ means all parts of an ecosystem are connected — changing one affects others.
questions.explanation: Interdependence is a key geographical concept — ecosystems are complex, interconnected systems.

questions.question: The ________ floor receives less than 2% of sunlight — plants here have large, dark leaves.
questions.explanation: Large, dark leaves are an adaptation to capture maximum light in the very dark forest floor.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: found near equator — hot, wet, high biodiversity
variety of species — highest in tropical rainforests
clearing forest — habitat loss, carbon release, soil erosion
nutrients in biomass — not in soil
meeting needs without compromising future generations

cards.definition: the increasing proportion of people living in urban areas — a global trend driven by rural-urban migration
movement of people from rural areas to cities — driven by push factors (poverty, lack of services) and pull factors (jobs, services)
a city with a population of over 10 million — most megacities are now in LEDCs
an area of poor housing — built illegally on land the residents don't own — often on city outskirts in LEDCs
a reason that drives people away from rural areas — e.g. poverty, drought, lack of education
a reason that attracts people to cities — e.g. jobs, better housing, healthcare, education
a lack of access to services and resources — linked to poverty and inequality in cities
the uncontrolled outward growth of cities into surrounding areas
previously developed urban land that is available for redevelopment — preferred over greenfield
land that has not been previously developed — often on the urban fringe; more controversial to build on

questions.question: What is urbanisation?
options: A) Movement of people from cities to rural areas
B) The increasing proportion of people living in urban areas
C) Building new cities in rural locations
D) The growth of agricultural land
questions.explanation: Urbanisation is the process by which an increasing proportion of a country's population lives in urban areas. It is driven by natural increase and rural-urban migration.

questions.question: What is a megacity?
options: A) A city with the world's largest buildings
B) A city with a population over 10 million
C) Any city in an LEDC
D) A city that has grown rapidly in the last decade
questions.explanation: Megacities are defined by population — over 10 million people. Most are now in LEDCs in Asia, Africa, and Latin America.

questions.question: What is a pull factor for rural-urban migration?
options: A) Drought and crop failure
B) Lack of schools in rural areas
C) Better job opportunities and services in cities
D) Poor quality housing in rural areas
questions.explanation: Pull factors draw people to cities — employment, higher wages, better healthcare, education, and entertainment.

questions.question: What is a squatter settlement?
options: A) Social housing built by governments
B) Illegal housing built on land residents don't own — usually city outskirts in LEDCs
C) Planned housing developments
D) High-rise accommodation in city centres
questions.explanation: Squatter settlements (like favelas in Brazil) form as migrants can't afford proper housing — they build on unused land, often with no legal rights.

questions.question: What is a brownfield site?
options: A) Land on the urban fringe
B) Agricultural land converted to housing
C) Previously developed land available for redevelopment
D) Greenspace in city centres
questions.explanation: Brownfield sites are previously built-on urban land — redeveloping them is preferred as it doesn't use green space.

questions.question: What are push factors for rural-urban migration?
options: A) High wages and good services in cities
B) Attractive natural environment in cities
C) Poverty, drought, lack of services, and unemployment in rural areas
D) Low cost of living in cities
questions.explanation: Push factors drive people away from rural areas — crop failure, natural disasters, poverty, lack of education or healthcare.

questions.question: What is urban sprawl?
options: A) Tall buildings in city centres
B) Uncontrolled outward growth of cities into surrounding areas
C) High density housing in urban areas
D) The migration of people to cities
questions.explanation: Urban sprawl is the spread of low-density development outward from city centres into surrounding countryside — often consuming greenfield land.

questions.question: Why are megacities mostly in LEDCs?
options: A) LEDCs have better city planning
B) Rapid urbanisation in LEDCs as people migrate for economic opportunities
C) MEDCs are declining in population
D) Megacities are defined differently in MEDCs
questions.explanation: As LEDCs industrialise, millions move to cities — rapid urbanisation creates megacities. MEDCs urbanised earlier and more gradually.

questions.question: What is social deprivation?
options: A) Having too many social opportunities
B) A lack of access to services, resources, and opportunities — linked to poverty
C) Living in a rural area
D) High crime rates in urban areas
questions.explanation: Social deprivation includes lack of access to education, healthcare, good housing, and employment — concentrated in the poorest urban areas.

questions.question: Why are greenfield sites controversial to build on?
options: A) They are expensive to develop
B) They lack transport links
C) They involve building on undeveloped countryside — destroying habitats and farmland
D) They are usually contaminated
questions.explanation: Greenfield sites are on the urban fringe or in the countryside — building on them destroys natural habitats and agricultural land. Brownfield development is preferred.

questions.question: Urbanisation is the movement of people from cities to rural areas.
questions.explanation: Urbanisation is the INCREASING proportion of people living in urban areas — the opposite of rural-to-urban migration.

questions.question: Most megacities are now located in LEDCs.
questions.explanation: Rapid industrialisation and rural-urban migration in LEDCs have created megacities — especially in Asia.

questions.question: A brownfield site is undeveloped land on the urban fringe.
questions.explanation: A brownfield site is previously developed urban land. A greenfield site is undeveloped land.

questions.question: Pull factors attract people to cities.
questions.explanation: Pull factors include better jobs, higher wages, education, healthcare, and entertainment — all drawing people to urban areas.

questions.question: Squatter settlements are usually found in the city centre.
questions.explanation: Squatter settlements are typically on the outskirts of cities in LEDCs — on land that is unused but legally owned by others.

questions.question: Social deprivation is linked to poverty and inequality.
questions.explanation: Social deprivation — lack of access to services and opportunities — is concentrated in the poorest urban areas.

questions.question: Urban sprawl increases the density of city centres.
questions.explanation: Urban sprawl is LOW-density outward growth — it expands city boundaries into surrounding areas.

questions.question: Redeveloping brownfield sites is preferred to building on greenfield sites.
questions.explanation: Brownfield redevelopment uses previously built-on urban land — it doesn't destroy green space or countryside.

questions.question: Rural-urban migration is driven by both push and pull factors.
questions.explanation: People are pushed from rural areas (poverty, lack of services) and pulled to cities (jobs, better services).

questions.question: Megacities are defined as cities with more than 1 million people.
questions.explanation: Megacities are defined as having more than 10 million people.

questions.question: ________ is the increasing proportion of people living in urban areas.
questions.explanation: Urbanisation is a global trend — driven by natural increase and rural-urban migration.

questions.question: A ________ is a city with a population over 10 million.
questions.explanation: Most megacities are now in LEDCs — especially in Asia and Africa.

questions.question: ________ factors push people away from rural areas — e.g. poverty and drought.
questions.explanation: Push factors include crop failure, lack of services, unemployment, and natural disasters.

questions.question: ________ factors attract people to cities — e.g. better jobs and services.
questions.explanation: Pull factors include higher wages, better education, healthcare, and entertainment.

questions.question: A ________ settlement is built illegally on land residents don't own.
questions.explanation: Squatter settlements (favelas, shanty towns) grow rapidly as migrants cannot afford legal housing.

questions.question: A ________ site is previously developed urban land — preferred for redevelopment.
questions.explanation: Brownfield development avoids using green space or destroying habitats.

questions.question: A ________ site is undeveloped land — more controversial to build on.
questions.explanation: Greenfield development destroys habitats and farmland — it is less sustainable.

questions.question: ________ sprawl is the uncontrolled outward growth of cities into surrounding areas.
questions.explanation: Urban sprawl consumes greenfield land and increases car dependency.

questions.question: Social ________ is a lack of access to services, resources, and opportunities.
questions.explanation: Social deprivation is concentrated in the poorest areas of cities.

questions.question: ________ migration is the movement from rural areas to cities.
questions.explanation: Rural-urban migration is driven by push factors (rural) and pull factors (urban).

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: increasing proportion of people in urban areas
city with 10 million+ population
movement from countryside to cities — push and pull factors
illegal housing on city outskirts in LEDCs
previously developed land — preferred for redevelopment

cards.definition: anything from the environment that meets human needs — water, food, energy
when demand for water exceeds available supply — a growing global problem
when people do not have reliable access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food
the amount of food energy consumed — varies hugely between HICs and LICs
when water supply exceeds demand — typically in wet, less-populated regions
when water demand exceeds supply — typically in hot, dry, or densely populated regions
the hidden water used to produce goods — e.g. 1 kg of beef requires ~15,000 litres of water
methods of food production that meet current needs without depleting resources for the future
the distance food travels from production to consumer — long food miles increase carbon emissions
large-scale commercial farming — uses technology and machinery to maximise yield — can be unsustainable

questions.question: What is water stress?
options: A) The weight of water on the landscape
B) When demand for water exceeds supply
C) When water quality decreases
D) When rivers flood regularly
questions.explanation: Water stress occurs when demand exceeds available supply. It is a growing problem — climate change, population growth, and economic development are increasing water demand.

questions.question: What is food insecurity?
options: A) Food being expensive
B) Unreliable access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food
C) A lack of variety in diet
D) Food being unsafe to eat
questions.explanation: Food insecurity means people cannot rely on getting enough safe, nutritious food. Causes include poverty, conflict, drought, political instability, and climate change.

questions.question: Why is calorie consumption unequal globally?
options: A) Some countries have more farmland
B) HICs overconsume while many LICs suffer food insecurity — linked to wealth inequality
C) Certain climates cannot grow enough food
D) Population density determines food access
questions.explanation: There is massive global inequality in calorie consumption — people in HICs often consume excess calories while millions in LICs face starvation.

questions.question: What is virtual water?
options: A) Water used in computer data centres
B) The hidden water used to produce a good — e.g. 15,000 litres per kg of beef
C) Water that evaporates before it can be used
D) Underground water sources
questions.explanation: Virtual water is the water embedded in products — when countries import food they are indirectly importing water. It is a useful concept for understanding global water use.

questions.question: What is a water deficit?
options: A) Countries with more water than they need
B) When water supply exceeds demand
C) When demand for water exceeds available supply
D) Underground water storage
questions.explanation: Water deficit regions include the Middle East and parts of Africa — where hot, dry climates and growing populations create a severe mismatch between supply and demand.

questions.question: What is sustainable food production?
options: A) Maximising crop yields using fertilisers and pesticides
B) Food production that meets present needs without depleting future resources
C) Only growing food locally
D) Organic farming on small plots
questions.explanation: Sustainable food production aims to feed the world's growing population without destroying the natural systems that make food production possible.

questions.question: What are food miles?
options: A) The distance a farmer walks while harvesting
B) The distance food travels from production to consumer
C) The amount of food wasted per household
D) The cost of transporting food per kilometre
questions.explanation: Long food miles mean more carbon emissions from transport. Locally grown food has lower food miles and a smaller carbon footprint.

questions.question: What is agribusiness?
options: A) A type of sustainable farming
B) Small-scale subsistence farming
C) Large-scale commercial farming using technology to maximise yield
D) Farming controlled by governments
questions.explanation: Agribusiness uses technology, machinery, and economies of scale to maximise yield — but can deplete soil, cause water pollution, and reduce biodiversity.

questions.question: Which region typically has water surplus?
options: A) Middle East
B) North Africa
C) Northern Europe and Canada
D) Sub-Saharan Africa
questions.explanation: Regions with high rainfall and lower population density — like northern Europe and Canada — typically have water surplus.

questions.question: How does climate change affect food security?
options: A) It has no effect on food production
B) It increases crop yields globally
C) It causes more frequent droughts, floods, and extreme weather — threatening crop yields
D) It makes farming easier in cold regions
questions.explanation: Climate change threatens food security — changing rainfall patterns, more extreme weather events, and rising temperatures affect crop yields, especially in LICs.

questions.question: Water stress occurs when supply exceeds demand.
questions.explanation: Water stress occurs when DEMAND exceeds supply — the opposite.

questions.question: Food insecurity means people cannot reliably access enough safe, nutritious food.
questions.explanation: Food insecurity is a major global challenge — affecting hundreds of millions of people.

questions.question: Virtual water refers to water used directly in drinking.
questions.explanation: Virtual water is the hidden water used to produce goods — e.g. 1 kg of beef requires ~15,000 litres.

questions.question: Calorie consumption is roughly equal across all countries.
questions.explanation: There is massive global inequality — HICs often overconsume while many LIC populations face starvation.

questions.question: Sustainable food production meets current needs without depleting future resources.
questions.explanation: Sustainability means meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.

questions.question: Long food miles reduce carbon emissions.
questions.explanation: Long food miles INCREASE carbon emissions — more fuel is used in transporting food long distances.

questions.question: Agribusiness uses technology to maximise crop yields.
questions.explanation: Agribusiness is large-scale commercial farming using machinery, pesticides, and fertilisers to maximise output.

questions.question: Water deficit regions include the Middle East and North Africa.
questions.explanation: These regions have high temperatures, low rainfall, and growing populations — creating severe water deficits.

questions.question: Climate change increases food security by making cold regions more productive.
questions.explanation: While some cold regions may benefit, overall climate change THREATENS food security through more droughts, floods, and extreme weather.

questions.question: Food miles are the distance food travels from farm to consumer.
questions.explanation: Reducing food miles by buying locally grown produce reduces the carbon footprint of our diet.

questions.question: ________ stress occurs when demand for water exceeds available supply.
questions.explanation: Water stress is increasing globally due to population growth, climate change, and economic development.

questions.question: Food ________ means unreliable access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food.
questions.explanation: Food insecurity affects hundreds of millions of people — caused by poverty, conflict, and climate change.

questions.question: ________ water is the hidden water embedded in products.
questions.explanation: 1 kg of beef requires approximately 15,000 litres of virtual water.

questions.question: ________ food production meets current needs without depleting resources for the future.
questions.explanation: Sustainable methods include organic farming, crop rotation, and reducing food waste.

questions.question: ________ miles are the distance food travels from production to consumer.
questions.explanation: Longer food miles mean more transport, more carbon emissions, and greater climate impact.

questions.question: Water ________ regions have more water than they need.
questions.explanation: Northern Europe and Canada typically have water surplus due to high rainfall and lower population density.

questions.question: Water ________ regions have more demand than supply — e.g. Middle East, North Africa.
questions.explanation: Water deficit is increasing — driven by population growth, climate change, and irrigation.

questions.question: ________ is large-scale commercial farming using technology to maximise yield.
questions.explanation: Agribusiness can be unsustainable — it may deplete soil and reduce biodiversity.

questions.question: ________ change threatens food security through droughts, floods, and extreme weather.
questions.explanation: Climate change is making food production more unpredictable — especially in LICs.

questions.question: Global ________ consumption is unequal — HICs consume excess while LICs face hunger.
questions.explanation: Inequality in calorie consumption reflects wider economic inequality between HICs and LICs.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: demand exceeds supply
unreliable access to safe, nutritious food
hidden water embedded in products
meets present needs without depleting future resources
distance food travels — long = more carbon emissions
